title: The 25 Best Movies of the Century: No. 23 - ‘Something’s Gotta Give’
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins put Nancy Meyers's Something's Gotta Give at No. 23, the one spot on their 25-for-25 list where they went off script. They call it the last great studio romantic comedy and walk through why: the Keaton and Nicholson personas colliding, the Jack character as a barely-veiled Warren Beatty, the $124 million box office, the Nancy Meyerscore that started here, and whether Erica should have just picked Keanu.

films.title: Something's Gotta Give
films.note: The pick, at No. 23. It's the first spot the hosts went off script on, bumping it down from 22. Written and directed by Nancy Meyers; Diane Keaton as playwright Erica Barry and Jack Nicholson as hip-hop label owner Harry Sanborn, with Keanu Reeves as the Hamptons doctor, Amanda Peet as Erica's daughter Marin, and Frances McDormand. Made $124M domestic and roughly $265M worldwide off a December release. Sean and Amanda read the Nicholson character as an almost literal Warren Beatty (the Joan Collins and Diane Sawyer name-drops, 'the escape artist') and Erica as a Meyers stand-in writing her own life into a play. Sean calls it the last great studio romantic comedy and the film that codified 'Nancy Meyerscore'; both keep circling the Keanu 'should she just pick him' question and Nicholson's late-career mortality read (multiple heart attacks, the hospital-gown gag).

films.title: The Intern
films.note: Amanda's beloved Meyers, with De Niro as a retiree interning under Anne Hathaway and falling for Rene Russo's masseuse. Sean grants it's very good but says Something's Gotta Give is the better, more self-contained film.

films.title: Baby Boom
films.note: Written by Meyers, directed by then-husband Charles Shyer, starring Diane Keaton; Sean notes The Intern basically plays as its sequel.

films.title: What Women Want
films.note: The Meyers hit that cleared $100M domestic, asking its title question a few films before this one.

films.title: It's Complicated
films.note: Described as this movie remade with different Oscar winners: Meryl Streep runs the prepared-foods store and chooses between ex-husband Alec Baldwin (the Jack part) and architect Steve Martin. Both hosts like it but call it more flawed. Sean's read: Steve Martin just isn't as sexy, so it lacks the heat.

films.title: The Holiday
films.note: The other Meyers title fans might have wanted in this slot; the hosts rank it well below and joke it's a 14-hour miniseries, 'Kieslowski's The Decalogue.'

films.title: Irreconcilable Differences
films.note: Meyers-written and the hardest of her films to see; a thinly veiled Peter Bogdanovich/Polly Platt split, with Drew Barrymore as a child suing to divorce her parents, something Barrymore later pursued in real life.

films.title: Father of the Bride
films.note: The Shyer/Meyers hit with Keaton second-billed to Steve Martin.

films.title: Father of the Bride Part II
films.note: The 1995 hit; Amanda fixates on the workout scene with Martin Short as all she could think about through two pregnancies.

films.title: The Parent Trap
films.note: Meyers's remake, cited among her run of studio hits.

films.title: Annie Hall
films.note: The Keaton persona this film riffs on, and the first of the if-you-liked-this recommendations.

films.title: The First Wives Club
films.note: Keaton's massive mid-'90s success and the high point before her late-'90s and early-2000s box-office lull.

films.title: Marvin's Room
films.note: Keaton's Oscar-bid ensemble with Leonardo DiCaprio, De Niro and Meryl Streep, a stacked cast but not a big hit.

films.title: The Only Thrill
films.note: The Peter Masterson film from the middle of Keaton's down period.

films.title: The Other Sister
films.note: Another Keaton title from that late-'90s stretch.

films.title: Hanging Up
films.note: Keaton directs, from a Nora and Delia Ephron script.

films.title: Town & Country
films.note: The famous bomb: Warren Beatty, Keaton, Andie MacDowell and Garry Shandling from a Buck Henry script; Keaton's reunion with Beatty two years before this movie.

films.title: Reds
films.note: Keaton and Nicholson's first screen pairing, Nicholson as Eugene O'Neill; Sean singles out their breakup scene as one of his favorite in movie history.

films.title: About Schmidt
films.note: Nicholson's rejection of the Jack persona and the film right before this one, where, the hosts say, he 'jacks very hard.'

films.title: The Departed
films.note: The later-Nicholson role, a few years after this.

films.title: The Bucket List
films.note: Another late Nicholson title in the run to his final films.

films.title: How Do You Know
films.note: Nicholson's last film to date, which Sean tags as the most recent James L. Brooks movie until Brooks's return this fall.

films.title: As Good as It Gets
films.note: A Brooks/Nicholson collaboration cited in the pair's long working history.

films.title: Broadcast News
films.note: Another Brooks/Nicholson film, and floated as a recommendation for the Brooks and Nicholson overlap.

films.title: Terms of Endearment
films.note: The Brooks/Nicholson classic in that filmography.

films.title: Spanglish
films.note: The Brooks film Nicholson is not in; Sean admits he really doesn't like it, which pains him because he loves Brooks and Adam Sandler.

films.title: Bridget Jones's Diary
films.note: First of Sean's list of romantic comedies that didn't make the 25 and that he argues Something's Gotta Give transcends.

films.title: How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days
films.note: One of the rom-coms Sean names as not making the list.

films.title: 13 Going on 30
films.note: Another rom-com cited among the list's near-misses.

films.title: 27 Dresses
films.note: On Sean's roll call of romantic comedies that didn't make the cut.

films.title: The Wedding Planner
films.note: Another of the rom-coms Sean lists as left off.

films.title: The Proposal
films.note: Cited among the romantic comedies that didn't make the 25.

films.title: Forgetting Sarah Marshall
films.note: The last of Sean's near-miss rom-coms.

films.title: The Philadelphia Story
films.note: The screwball touchstone Amanda reaches for, with its three-in-a-nice-house structure and Keanu as the Cary Grant/Jimmy Stewart interloper who comes to town.

films.title: Bringing Up Baby
films.note: One of the '30s and '40s screwball models Meyers echoes in the film's framework.

films.title: His Girl Friday
films.note: Another screwball classic the hosts hear in this movie's DNA.

films.title: American Pie
films.note: Cited as the film that kicked off the post-1999 MILF vogue that this one twists into 'young man hot for older woman.'

films.title: Black Bag
films.note: A recent show topic, brought up as steamy-but-chaste next to how physical Something's Gotta Give is willing to be.

films.title: You've Got Mail
films.note: The Nora Ephron recommendation and its AIM/email romance, the next film on Sean's list.

films.title: Heartburn
films.note: An Ephron-written recommendation for more 'dastardly Jack.'

films.title: Julie & Julia
films.note: The Ephron title Sean calls half-working: eligible, but only Julia's half really lands for him.

films.title: Lucky Numbers
films.note: Floated as a possible Nora Ephron title while sorting through her filmography (she directed it).

films.title: John Wick
films.note: The Keanu punchline: a wonderful doctor in the Hamptons with no reconstructive knee surgery or bullet holes to speak of.

films.title: A Minecraft Movie
films.note: The throwaway example of a disposable hit ('made $300 million') against this evergreen pick.

films.title: Warfare
films.note: Teased for the next episode: the combat film Sean and Chris Ryan will discuss after the Cannes preview.

Father of the Bride Part II.title: Father of the Bride Part II

How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days.title: How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days
